content: If $x=\tan ^{-1}\left\{\frac{\sqrt{1+t^2}-1}{t}\right\}, y=\cos ^{-1}\left\{\frac{1-t^2}{1+t^2}\right\}, \quad$ then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$ is equal to
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & x=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+\mathrm{t}^2}-1}{\mathrm{t}}\right) \\ & \text { Put } \mathrm{t}=\tan \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{t} \\ & \therefore \quad x=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+\tan ^2 \theta}-1}{\tan \theta}\right) \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sec \theta-1}{\tan \theta}\right) \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-\cos \theta}{\sin \theta}\right) \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=\tan ^{-1}\left(\tan \frac{\theta}{2}\right) \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,=\frac{\theta}{2}=\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} \mathrm{t} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{1}{2\left(1+\mathrm{t}^2\right)} \\ & y=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-\mathrm{t}^2}{1+\mathrm{t}^2}\right) \\ & \text { Put } \mathrm{t}=\tan \theta \Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1} \mathrm{t} \\ & \therefore \quad y=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-\tan ^2 \theta}{1+\tan ^2 \theta}\right) \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =\cos ^{-1}(\cos 2 \theta) \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =2 \theta \\ &\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\, =2 \tan ^{-1} \mathrm{t} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{2}{1+\mathrm{t}^2} \\ \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{dt}}}{\frac{\mathrm{~d} x}{\mathrm{dt}}}=\frac{\frac{2}{1+\mathrm{t}^2}}{\frac{1}{2\left(1+\mathrm{t}^2\right)}}=4 \end{array} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \cot ^{-1}\left(2 \cdot 1^2\right)+\cot ^{-1}\left(2 \cdot 2^2\right)+\cot ^{-1}\left(2 \cdot 3^2\right)+\ldots \ldots \ldots \infty \\ & =\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\infty} \cot ^{-1}\left(2 \mathrm{r}^2\right) \\ & =\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\infty} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2 \mathrm{r}^2}\right) \\ & =\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\infty} \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{(1+2 \mathrm{r})+(1-2 \mathrm{r})}{1-(1+2 \mathrm{r})(1-2 \mathrm{r})}\right] \\ & =\sum_{\mathrm{r}=1}^{\infty}\left[\tan ^{-1}(1+2 \mathrm{r})+\tan ^{-1}(1-2 \mathrm{r})\right] \\ & =\tan ^{-1} 3+\tan ^{-1}(-1)+\tan ^{-1} 5+\tan ^{-1}(-3) +\tan ^{-1} 7+\tan ^{-1}(-5)+\ldots+\tan ^{-1} \infty \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} =\tan ^{-1} 3-\tan ^{-1} 1+\tan ^{-1} 5 & -\tan ^{-1} 3+\tan ^{-1} 7 -\tan ^{-1} 5+\ldots+\tan ^{-1} \infty \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\tan ^{-1} \infty-\tan ^{-1} 1 \\ & =\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{\pi}{4} \\ & =\frac{\pi}{4} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & 2 \tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{5}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{2}{5}}{1-\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^2}\right) \\ & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\quad \ldots\left[\because 2 \tan ^{-1} x=\tan ^{-1} \frac{2 x}{1-x^2}\right] \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{2}{5}}{\frac{24}{25}}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{5}{12}\right) \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \tan \left(2 \tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{5}-\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \\ & =\tan \left[\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{5}{12}\right)-\tan ^{-1} 1\right] \\ & =\tan \left[\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{5}{12}-1}{1+\left(\frac{5}{12}\right)(1)}\right)\right] \\ & =\tan \left[\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{-7}{17}\right)\right] \\ & =\frac{-7}{17} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $\cot ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})-\tan ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})=x$, then the value of $\sin x$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \cot ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})-\tan ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})=x \\ \Rightarrow & \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{\cos \alpha}}\right)-\tan ^{-1}(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})=x \\ \Rightarrow & \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{\cos \alpha}}-\sqrt{\cos \alpha}}{1+\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{\cos \alpha}}\right)(\sqrt{\cos \alpha})}\right)=x \\ \Rightarrow & \tan x=\frac{1-\cos \alpha}{2 \sqrt{\cos \alpha}} \\ \therefore & \sin x=\frac{1-\cos \alpha}{1+\cos \alpha}=\frac{2 \sin ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}}{2 \cos ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2}}=\tan ^2 \frac{\alpha}{2} \end{aligned} $$

content: Considering only the principal values of the inverse trigonometric functions, the value of $\tan \left(\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)-2 \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}\right)\right)$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{4}\right) & \\ \ldots & {\left[\because \sin ^{-1} x=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right)\right] } \end{aligned} $$$$ \begin{aligned} 2 \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}\right)=2 \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) & \\ & \ldots\left[\because \cos ^{-1} x=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{x}\right)\right] \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} 2 \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) \\ & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\ldots\left[\because 2 \tan ^{-1} x=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{1-x^2}\right)\right] \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \tan \left(\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)-2 \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}\right)\right) \\ = & \tan \left(\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)-\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)\right) \\ = & \tan \left(\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{3}{4}-\frac{4}{3}}{1+\left(\frac{3}{4}\right)\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)}\right)\right) \\ = & \tan \left(\tan ^{-1}\left(-\frac{7}{24}\right)\right)=-\frac{7}{24} \end{aligned} $$

then the value of $y^{\prime}(0)$ is

$$ \begin{array}{cc} & =\tan ^{-1}(x+3)-\tan ^{-1} x \\ \therefore \quad & y^{\prime}=\frac{1}{1+(x+3)^2}-\frac{1}{1+x^2} \\ \therefore \quad & y^{\prime}(0)=\frac{1}{1+(0+3)^2}-\frac{1}{1+0^2} \\ & =\frac{1}{10}-1=\frac{-9}{10} \end{array} $$

content: If $\sin ^{-1}(4 x)+\sin ^{-1}(4 \sqrt{3} x)=-\frac{\pi}{2}$, then the value of $x$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Let } \sin ^{-1}(4 x)=\alpha \text { and } \sin ^{-1}(4 \sqrt{3} x)=\beta\\ &\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \sin \alpha=4 x \text { and } \sin \beta=4 \sqrt{3} x \\ & \alpha+\beta=\frac{-\pi}{2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \beta=\frac{-\pi}{2}-\alpha \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \beta=\sin \left(\frac{-\pi}{2}-\alpha\right)=-\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}+\alpha\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \beta=-\cos \alpha \\ & \Rightarrow \cos \alpha=-4 \sqrt{3} x \\ & \text { Now, } \cos \alpha=\sqrt{1-\sin ^2 \alpha} \\ & \Rightarrow-4 \sqrt{3} x=\sqrt{1-16 x^2} \end{aligned} $$

Squaring both sides, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} & 48 x^2=1-16 x^2 \\ & \Rightarrow 64 x^2=1 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2=\frac{1}{64} \Rightarrow x= \pm \frac{1}{8} \end{aligned} $$

For $x=\frac{1}{8}$,

$$ \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)+\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)=\frac{\pi}{6}+\frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{\pi}{2} \neq \frac{-\pi}{2} $$

For $x=\frac{-1}{8}$,

$$ \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{-1}{2}\right)+\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)=\frac{-\pi}{6}-\frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{-\pi}{2} $$

$\therefore \quad x=\frac{-1}{8}$ is the required solution.

content: The number of positive integral solutions of $\tan ^{-1} x+\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{\sqrt{1+y^2}}\right)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{10}}\right)$ are

$$ \Rightarrow \tan \theta=\frac{1}{y} \Rightarrow \theta=\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{y} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{\sqrt{10}}\right)=\phi \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \phi=\frac{3}{\sqrt{10}} \\ & \Rightarrow \tan \phi=\frac{3}{1}=3 \\ & \Rightarrow \phi=\tan ^{-1} 3 \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)^2+\left(\cot ^{-1} x\right)^2=\frac{5 \pi^2}{8} \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\tan ^{-1} x+\cot ^{-1} x\right)^2-2 \tan ^{-1} x \cot ^{-1} x=\frac{5 \pi^2}{8} \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\tan ^{-1} x+\cot ^{-1} x\right)^2 -2 \tan ^{-1} x\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\tan ^{-1} x\right)=\frac{5 \pi^2}{8} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{\pi^2}{4}-2 \times \frac{\pi}{2} \tan ^{-1} x+2\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)^2=\frac{5 \pi^2}{8} \\ & \Rightarrow 2\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)^2-\pi \tan ^{-1} x-\frac{3 \pi^2}{8}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \tan ^{-1} x=-\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{3 \pi}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \tan ^{-1} x=-\frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow x=-1 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2+1=2 \end{aligned} $$

content: If $y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4 x}{1+5 x^2}\right)+\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{3-2 x}{2+3 x}\right)$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$ is equal to
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} y & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4 x}{1+5 x^2}\right)+\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{3-2 x}{2+3 x}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4 x}{1+5 x^2}\right)+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2+3 x}{3-2 x}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{5 x-x}{1+5 x(x)}\right)+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{2}{3}+x}{1-\frac{2}{3} x}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}(5 x)-\tan ^{-1} x+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)+\tan ^{-1} x \\ & =\tan ^{-1}(5 x)+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right) \\ \therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x} & =\frac{1}{1+(5 x)^2} \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(5 x)=\frac{5}{1+25 x^2} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $0 \leqslant \cos ^{-1} x \leqslant \pi$ and $\frac{-\pi}{2} \leqslant \sin ^{-1} x \leqslant \frac{\pi}{2}$, then at $x=\frac{1}{5}$ the value of $\cos \left(2 \cos ^{-1} x+\sin ^{-1} x\right)$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \cos \left(2 \cos ^{-1} x+\sin ^{-1} x\right) \\ & =\cos \left[\left(\sin ^{-1} x+\cos ^{-1} x\right)+\cos ^{-1} x\right] \\ & =\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}+\cos ^{-1} x\right) \\ & =-\sin \left(\cos ^{-1} x\right) \\ & =-\sin \left(\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{\left(1-x^2\right)}\right) \\ & =-\sqrt{1-x^2} \\ & =-\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^2}=-\sqrt{\frac{24}{25}} \end{aligned} $$

content: Considering only the principal values of the inverse trigonometric function, the value of $\tan \left(\cos ^{-1} \frac{1}{5 \sqrt{2}}-\sin ^{-1} \frac{4}{\sqrt{17}}\right)$ is
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \cos ^{-1} \frac{1}{5 \sqrt{2}}=\theta \Rightarrow \cos \theta=\frac{1}{5 \sqrt{2}} \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \theta=\frac{7}{5 \sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow \tan \theta=7 \\ & \text { Let } \sin ^{-1} \frac{4}{\sqrt{17}}=\phi \Rightarrow \sin \phi=\frac{4}{\sqrt{17}} \\ & \Rightarrow \cos \phi=\frac{1}{\sqrt{17}} \Rightarrow \tan \phi=4 \\ & \tan \left(\cos ^{-1} \frac{1}{5 \sqrt{2}}-\sin ^{-1} \frac{4}{\sqrt{17}}\right) \\ & =\tan (\theta-\phi)=\frac{\tan \theta-\tan \phi}{1+\tan \theta \tan \phi} \\ & =\frac{7-4}{1+28}=\frac{3}{29} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & \tan ^{-1}\left(x+\frac{2}{x}\right)-\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{x}\right)-\tan ^{-1}\left(x-\frac{2}{x}\right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left(x+\frac{2}{x}\right)-\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{x}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left(x-\frac{2}{x}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{x+\frac{2}{x}-\frac{4}{x}}{1+\left(x+\frac{2}{x}\right)\left(\frac{4}{x}\right)}\right]=\tan ^{-1}\left(x-\frac{2}{x}\right) \end{aligned} $$

One solution is $x-\frac{2}{x}=0$

content: The derivative of $\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+x^2}-1}{x}\right)$ w.r.t. $\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x \sqrt{1-x^2}}{1-2 x^2}\right)$ at $x=0$ is
explanation:
$$ \begin{gathered} \text { Let } y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+x^2}-1}{x}\right) \\ \quad \text { Put } x=\tan \theta \\ \therefore \quad y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+\tan ^2 \theta}-1}{\tan \theta}\right) \\ \quad=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sec \theta-1}{\tan \theta}\right) \\ \quad=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-\cos \theta}{\sin \theta}\right) \\ \quad=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \sin ^2 \frac{\theta}{2}}{2 \sin \frac{\theta}{2} \cos \frac{\theta}{2}}\right) \end{gathered} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\tan \frac{\theta}{2}\right)=\frac{\theta}{2}\\ &\text { Differentiating w.r.t. } \theta \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} \theta}=\frac{1}{2} \\ & \text { Let } \mathrm{z}=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x \sqrt{1-x^2}}{1-2 x^2}\right) \\ & \text { Put } x=\sin \theta \\ \therefore \quad & z=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \sin \theta \sqrt{1-\sin ^2 \theta}}{1-2 \sin ^2 \theta}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 \sin \theta \cos \theta}{\cos 2 \theta}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin 2 \theta}{\cos 2 \theta}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}(\tan 2 \theta) \\ \therefore \quad & z=2 \theta \end{array} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Differentiating w.r.t. } \theta \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{d z}{d \theta}=2 \\ \therefore \quad & \frac{d y}{d z}=\frac{\frac{d y}{d \theta}}{\frac{d z}{d \theta}}=\frac{1}{2 \cdot 2}=\frac{1}{4} \end{array} \end{aligned} $$

content: If $y=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}\right)+\sec ^{-1}\left(\frac{1+x^2}{1-x^2}\right)$ then the value of $\frac{d y}{d x}$ at $x=\sqrt{3}$ is

content: If $\sin \left(\sin ^{-1} \frac{1}{5}+\cos ^{-1} x\right)=1$, then the value of $x$ is
explanation:
Given:

$ \sin \left(\sin^{-1} \frac{1}{5} + \cos^{-1} x\right) = 1 $

Let us solve step by step:

Step 1: Let $\sin^{-1} \frac{1}{5} = \theta$.

So, $\sin \theta = \frac{1}{5}$

Step 2: Let $\cos^{-1} x = \phi$.

So, $\cos \phi = x$

The given equation becomes:

$ \sin(\theta+\phi) = 1 $

Step 3: The value of $\sin(\theta+\phi) = 1$

So,

$ \theta + \phi = \frac{\pi}{2} $

Step 4: Substitute back the values

$ \theta + \phi = \frac{\pi}{2} \implies \phi = \frac{\pi}{2} - \theta $

But, $\phi = \cos^{-1} x$. So,

$ \cos^{-1} x = \frac{\pi}{2} - \theta $

Step 5: Take cosines both sides

$ x = \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{2} - \theta\right) $

Recall: $\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{2} - \theta\right) = \sin \theta$

$ x = \sin \theta $

But we already have $\sin \theta = \frac{1}{5}$

So,

$ x = \frac{1}{5} $

Final Answer:

$ \boxed{\frac{1}{5}} $

So the correct option is (A).

content: If $\cos ^{-1} x+\cos ^{-1} y+\cos ^{-1} z=\pi$ and $x^2+y^2+z^2+k x y z=1$, then k is

content: If $y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3+2 x}{2-3 x}\right)+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3 x}{1+4 x^2}\right)$, then $\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}$ is equal to
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} y & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3+2 x}{2-3 x}\right)+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3 x}{1+4 x^2}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{3}{2}+x}{1-\frac{3}{2} x}\right)+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4 x-x}{1+(4 x)(x)}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)+\tan ^{-1} x+\tan ^{-1} 4 x-\tan ^{-1} x \\ \therefore \quad y & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)+\tan ^{-1}(4 x) \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Differentiating w.r.t. } x \text {, we get }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=0+\frac{4}{1+(4 x)^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{4}{1+16 x^2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

content: The number of real solutions of $\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{x(x+1)}+\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{x^2+x+1}=\frac{\pi}{2}$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & \tan ^{-1} \sqrt{x(x+1)}+\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{x^2+x+1}=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ & \tan ^{-1} \sqrt{x(x+1)} \text { is defined when } \\ & x(x+1) \geq 0 \quad\text{.... (i)}\\ & \sin ^{-1} \sqrt{x^2+x+1} \text { is defined when } \\ & x(x+1)+1 \leq 1 \text { or } x(x+1) \leq 0 \quad\text{.... (ii)} \end{aligned}\\ &\begin{aligned} & \text { From (i) and (ii), } \\ & x(x+1)=0 \Rightarrow x=0 \text { or }-1 . \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Hence, number of solutions is } 2 \text {. } \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } \frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{a}{b}\right)=\theta \\ & \therefore \quad \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{~b}}\right)=2 \theta \\ & \therefore \quad \cos 2 \theta=\frac{a}{b} \\ & \therefore \quad \tan \left[\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{~b}}\right)\right]+\tan \left[\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{~b}}\right)\right] \\ & =\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\theta\right)+\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\theta\right) \\ & =\frac{1+\tan \theta}{1-\tan \theta}+\frac{1-\tan \theta}{1+\tan \theta} \\ & =\frac{(1+\tan \theta)^2+(1-\tan \theta)^2}{1-\tan ^2 \theta} \\ & =\frac{2\left(1+\tan ^2 \theta\right)}{1-\tan ^2 \theta} \\ & =\frac{2}{\frac{1-\tan ^2 \theta}{1+\tan ^2 \theta}} \\ & =\frac{2}{\cos 2 \theta} \\ & =\frac{2}{\frac{a}{b}}=\frac{2 b}{a} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \tan \left(2 \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2}\right)\right) \\ & =\frac{2 \tan \left(\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2}\right)\right)}{1-\tan ^2\left(\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2}\right)\right)} \\ & =\frac{2\left(\frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2}\right)}{1-\left(\frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{2}\right)^2} \\ & =\frac{\sqrt{5}-1}{1-\left(\frac{6-2 \sqrt{5}}{4}\right)} \\ & =\frac{4(\sqrt{5}-1)}{2 \sqrt{5}-2} \\ & =\frac{4(\sqrt{5}-1)}{2(\sqrt{5}-1)}=2 \end{aligned}$$

content: Let $f(\theta)=\sin \left(\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin \theta}{\sqrt{\cos 2 \theta}}\right)\right)$, where $\frac{-\pi}{4}<\theta<\frac{\pi}{4}$, then the value of $\frac{d}{d(\tan \theta)}(f(\theta))$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & f(\theta)=\sin \left(\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin \theta}{\sqrt{\cos 2 \theta}}\right)\right) \\ &=\sin \left(\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin \theta}{\sqrt{\cos 2 \theta+\sin ^2 \theta}}\right)\right) \\ & \ldots\left[\because \tan ^{-1} x=\sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{\sqrt{1+x^2}}\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & =\sin \left(\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin \theta}{\sqrt{\cos ^2 \theta-\sin ^2 \theta+\sin ^2 \theta}}\right)\right) \\ & =\sin \left(\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin \theta}{\sqrt{\cos ^2 \theta}}\right)\right) \\ & =\sin \left(\sin ^{-1}(\tan \theta)\right) \\ & =\tan \theta \end{aligned}$$

$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}(\tan \theta)}(\mathrm{f}(\theta))=1$

content: The value of $\cos \left(2 \cos ^{-1} x+\sin ^{-1} x\right)$ at $x=\frac{1}{5}$ where $0 \leq \cos ^{-1} x \leq \pi$ and $-\frac{\pi}{2} \leq \sin ^{-1} x \leq \frac{\pi}{2}$, is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \cos \left(2 \cos ^{-1} x+\sin ^{-1} x\right) \\ & =\cos \left[\left(\sin ^{-1} x+\cos ^{-1} x\right)+\cos ^{-1} x\right] \\ & =\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2}+\cos ^{-1} x\right) \\ & =-\sin \left(\cos ^{-1} x\right) \\ & =-\sin \left(\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{\left(1-x^2\right)}\right) \\ & =-\sqrt{1-x^2} \\ & =-\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^2} \\ & =-\sqrt{\frac{24}{25}}=-\frac{2 \sqrt{6}}{5} \end{aligned}$$

content:
Considering only the principal values of inverse function, the set

$$A=\left\{x \geq 0 / \tan ^{-1}(2 x)+\tan ^{-1}(3 x)=\frac{\pi}{4}\right\}$$
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \tan ^{-1}(2 x)+\tan ^{-1}(3 x)=\frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left[\frac{2 x+3 x}{1-(2 x)(3 x)}\right]=\frac{\pi}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{5 x}{1-6 x^2}=\tan \frac{\pi}{4}=1 \\ & \Rightarrow 6 x^2+5 x-1=0 \\ & \Rightarrow(x+1)(6 x-1)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow x=-1 \text { or } x=\frac{1}{6} \\ & \text { But, } x \geq 0 \\ & \therefore \quad x=\frac{1}{6} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{~A} \text { is a singleton set. } \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\cos ^{-1} x-\cos ^{-1} \frac{y}{3}=\alpha$$, where $$-1 \leq x \leq 1$, $-3 \leq y \leq 3, x \leq \frac{y}{3}$$, then for all $$x, y, 9 x^2-6 x y \cos \alpha+y^2$$ is equal to
explanation:
If $$\cos ^{-1} \frac{x}{a}-\cos ^{-1} \frac{y}{b}=\theta$$, then

$$\frac{x^2}{a^2}-\frac{2 x y}{a b} \cos \theta+\frac{y^2}{b^2}=\sin ^2 \theta$$

Given, $$\cos ^{-1} x-\cos ^{-1} \frac{y}{3}=\alpha$$

Here, $$a=1, b=3$$

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad & \frac{x^2}{1^2}-\frac{2 x y}{(1)(3)} \cos \alpha+\frac{y^2}{3^2}=\sin ^2 \alpha \\ & \Rightarrow x^2-\frac{2 x y}{3} \cos \alpha+\frac{y^2}{9}=\sin ^2 \alpha \\ & \Rightarrow 9 x^2-6 x y \cos \alpha+y^2=9 \sin ^2 \alpha \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{5}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{5}}{1-\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{5}}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{8}\right)+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{7}{9}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{1}{8}+\frac{7}{9}}{1-\frac{1}{8} \cdot \frac{7}{9}}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{65}{72}}{\frac{65}{72}}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}(1) \\ & =\frac{\pi}{4} \end{aligned}$$

content: Given $$0 \leq x \leq \frac{1}{2}$$, then the value of $$\tan \left(\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)-\sin ^{-1} x\right)$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \tan \left[\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{\sqrt{1-x^2}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)-\sin ^{-1} x\right] \\ & =\tan \left[\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+\sqrt{1-x^2}}{\sqrt{2}}\right)-\sin ^{-1} x\right] \\ & =\tan \left[\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sin \theta+\cos \theta}{\sqrt{2}}\right)-\theta\right] \quad \ldots\left[\begin{array}{l} \text { Put } \sin ^{-1} x=\theta \\ \Rightarrow x=\sin \theta \end{array}\right] \\ & =\tan \left[\sin ^{-1}\left[\sin \left(\theta+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right]-\theta\right] \\ & =\tan \left(\theta+\frac{\pi}{4}-\theta\right) \\ & =\tan \frac{\pi}{4}=1 \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)^2+\left(\cot ^{-1} x\right)^2=\frac{5 \pi^2}{8}$$, then the value of $$x$$ is
explanation:
We have, $$\tan ^{-1} x+\cot ^{-1} x=\pi / 2$$

The given equation can be written as

$$\begin{aligned} & \left(\tan ^{-1} x+\cot ^{-1} x\right)^2-2 \tan ^{-1} x\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\tan ^{-1} x\right)=\frac{5 \pi^2}{8} \\ & 2\left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)^2-2\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) \tan ^{-1}(x)-\frac{3 \pi^2}{8}=0 \\ & \tan ^{-1} x=-\frac{\pi}{4} \text { or } \frac{3 \pi}{4} \end{aligned}$$

Hence, $$\tan ^{-1} x=1 \Rightarrow x=-1$$

$$\begin{aligned} \sin ^{-1}\left(\sin \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right) & =\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) \\ = & \sin ^{-1}\left(\sin \frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\frac{\pi}{4} \quad\left[\because \frac{-\pi}{2} \leq \sin ^{-1} x \leq \frac{\pi}{2}\right] \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore \text { Principle value of } \sin ^{-1}\left(\sin \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right) \text { is } \frac{\pi}{4} \text {. }$$

content: $$x, y, z$$ are in G.P. and $$\tan ^{-1} x, \tan ^{-1} y, \tan ^{-1} z$$ are in A.P., then
explanation:

Also, $$\tan ^{-1} x, \tan ^{-1} y, \tan ^{-1} z$$ are in A.P.

$$\begin{aligned} & \tan ^{-1}(1+x)=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+(1+x)^2}}\right) \\ & \therefore \quad \sin \left(\cot ^{-1}(x)\right)=\cos \left(\tan ^{-1}(1+x)\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \sin \left(\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+x^2}}\right)\right)=\cos \left(\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+(1+x)^2}}\right)\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\sqrt{1+x^2}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{1+(1+x)^2}} \\ & \Rightarrow 1+(1+x)^2=1+x^2 \\ & \Rightarrow x=\frac{-1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \cos ^{-1} \sqrt{\mathrm{p}}-\cos ^{-1} \sqrt{1-\mathrm{p}}+\cos ^{-1} \sqrt{1-\mathrm{q}}=\frac{3 \pi}{4} \\ & \text { Let } \mathrm{t}=\cos ^{-1} \sqrt{\mathrm{p}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}=\cos ^2 \mathrm{t} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{p}=1-\sin ^2 \mathrm{t} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{sin} \mathrm{t}=\sqrt{1-\mathrm{p}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{1-\mathrm{p}} \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$\alpha=3 \sin ^{-1} \frac{6}{11}$$ and $$\beta=3 \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{9}\right)$$, where the inverse trigonometric functions take only the principal values, then the correct option is
explanation:

Since $$\frac{6}{11}>\frac{6}{12}$$

Taking $$\sin ^{-1}$$ on both sides, we get

(ii)} \end{aligned}$$

From (i) and (ii), we have $$\alpha$$ lies in II$$^{\text {nd }}$$ quadrant and $$\beta$$ lies in III$$^{\text {rd }}$$ quadrant.

Thus, $$\alpha+\beta$$ lies in IV$${ }^{\text {th }}$$ quadrant.

So, $$\cos (\alpha+\beta)>0$$

content: $$\text { If } y=\sqrt{\frac{1-\sin ^{-1}(x)}{1+\sin ^{-1}(x)}} \text {, then } \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x} \text { at } x=0 \text { and } y=1 \text { is }$$
explanation:
$$y=\sqrt{\frac{1-\sin ^{-1}(x)}{1+\sin ^{-1}(x)}}$$

Taking $$\log$$ on both sides, we get

$$\log y=\frac{1}{2}\left[\log \left(1-\sin ^{-1} x\right)-\log \left(1+\sin ^{-1} x\right)\right]$$

Differentiating w. r. t. $$x$$, we get

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{y} \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{1-\sin ^{-1} x} \cdot \frac{-1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}-\frac{1}{1+\sin ^{-1} x} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right] \\ & \frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}=\frac{-y}{2 \sqrt{1-x^2}}\left(\frac{1}{1-\sin ^{-1} x}+\frac{1}{1+\sin ^{-1} x}\right) \\ & \left(\frac{\mathrm{d} y}{\mathrm{~d} x}\right)_{(0,1)}=\frac{-1}{2(1)}\left(\frac{1}{1-0}+\frac{1}{1+0}\right)=-1 \end{aligned}$$

content: The domain of the function $$\mathrm{f}(x)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{|x|+5}{x^2+1}\right)$$ is $$(-\infty,-a] \cup[a, \infty)$$. Then a is equal to
explanation:
$$f(x)=\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{|x|+5}{x^2+1}\right)$$

Firstly, we are given that $$\sin^{-1}(0.8) = \theta$$, hence $$\sin(\theta) = 0.8$$. We now need to find $$\cos(\theta)$$ to use in the double angle formula.

Since sin and cos are related by the Pythagorean identity:

$$\sin^2(\theta) + \cos^2(\theta) = 1$$

We can solve for $$\cos(\theta)$$:

$$\cos^2(\theta) = 1 - \sin^2(\theta)$$

Substituting the value of $$\sin(\theta)$$:

$$\cos^2(\theta) = 1 - 0.8^2$$

$$\cos^2(\theta) = 1 - 0.64$$

The cosine has two possible values, +0.6 and -0.6, for angles in different quadrants, but since $$\theta = \sin^{-1}(0.8)$$ lies in the first quadrant (where sine and cosine are both positive), we select the positive value. Hence:

$$\cos(\theta) = 0.6$$

Now we can use the double angle formula for sine:

$$\sin(2\theta) = 2\sin(\theta)\cos(\theta) = 2 \cdot 0.8 \cdot 0.6 = 2 \cdot 0.48 = 0.96$$

Therefore, the value of $$\sin \left(2 \sin^{-1} 0.8\right)$$ is 0.96, which corresponds to Option A.

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { We have, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{3}+\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{5}+\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{7}+\tan ^{-1} \frac{1}{8} \\ & \Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{5}}{1-\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{5}}\right)+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{1}{7}+\frac{1}{8}}{1-\frac{1}{7} \times \frac{1}{8}}\right) \\ & \left(\because \tan ^{-1} x+\tan ^{-1} y=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+y}{1-x y}\right)\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{8}{14}\right)+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{15}{55}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{4}{7}\right)+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{11}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\frac{4}{7}+\frac{3}{11}}{1-\frac{4}{7} \times \frac{3}{11}}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{44+21}{77-12}\right) \\ & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{65}{65}\right)=\tan ^{-1}(1)=\frac{\pi}{4} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$
